moveAnnotations.annotation: We're starting with the English Opening, a flexible and strategic weapon that has been favored by world champions like Kasparov and Karpov. By moving the c-pawn two squares forward, we control the important d5 square and prepare to develop our pieces toward the center without committing to a d-pawn advance. This move sets up a kingside fianchetto while maintaining options like d4 later, giving us strategic flexibility and control of key central squares.

Black is playing the Jaenisch Gambit, an aggressive but risky counterattack against our c4 setup. By pushing the b-pawn forward, Black attacks our c4 pawn and tries to gain queenside space quickly, but this move weakens the a6-f1 diagonal and the queenside light squares significantly. We can accept this gambit with cxb5, leading to sharp tactical play where Black sacrifices material for active piece play and compensation—it's an interesting challenge that requires careful calculation.
